title: Meditations
author: Marcus Aurelius
lesson: In writing and reading you must learn before you teach.
notes:
**The problem**
I was curious about Stoicism. No dramatic reason.

**The concept**
Marcus wrote this for himself, not for readers. He kept repeating the same ideas across 12 books — not because he forgot, but because the repetition was the practice. What stuck with me: you can't be angry at someone who doesn't understand something you haven't explained clearly. His line is roughly "if he can't follow me, that's on me to explain better." I started applying that immediately.

**The decision**
I stopped caring about being right in arguments. I'm not looking for reasons that justify what I already think — I'm looking for what's true. Getting caught being wrong doesn't bother me. Changing my position when I'm shown something better is the point.

**Implementation**
Noticed the pattern "I'll be honest with you..." and stopped saying it. You should always be honest — flagging it as a special occasion is its own kind of dishonesty. I also dropped blaming circumstances for things I could control, and started treating meditation as a daily practice rather than something to do when things go sideways.

**Effect**
I watch behavior in people around me more clearly now — including my own — and I don't conflate the two. I can see something in someone else and recognise it without needing to call it out. I correct mine and move on.

**Trade-off**
Read it once and you'll underline things. Read it again and you'll find different ones. One pass isn't enough — the same sentence lands differently depending on where you are when you read it.

title: Think and Grow Rich
author: Napoleon Hill
lesson: Firstly desire then consistency in actions, the only price to pay is temporary failure.
notes:
**The problem**
You want things. Several of them. Vague, parallel, shifting. None of them move because wanting everything is the same as wanting nothing — diffuse energy doesn't build anything.

**The concept**
Burning desire as the ignition point. Hill isn't talking about motivation or positive thinking. He means a specific, written want with a deadline and a cost you've already agreed to pay. The 13 principles in this book — faith, autosuggestion, persistence, the Master Mind — all collapse without this one being real first. Desire isn't the fuel. It's the engine.

**The decision**
Write one goal. One. Specific, dated, with the price attached. Not a vision board. A contract.

**Implementation**
Read it twice a day — morning and night. Not as an affirmation ritual. As a way to keep the mind pointed at one direction instead of spreading across everything at once.

**Effect**
Scattered energy starts to consolidate. Small actions appear that weren't visible before because attention was split. The goal stops feeling abstract.

**Trade-off**
The book was written in 1937 and carries the blind spots of that era — the mysticism around "thought vibrations" and the unsupported certainty about how success works. Some chapters age poorly. The core principle — specificity of desire creates direction — is real and worth extracting. The rest you can filter out.

title: The Almanack of Naval Ravikant
author: Eric Jorgenson
lesson:
The greatest superpower is the ability to change yourself. 
Impatience with actions patience with results.
notes:
**The problem**
I found Naval through a podcast first. His way of reasoning and how he looks at the world pulled me in before I touched the book. The Almanack is essentially a compiled version of that — his thinking on wealth, happiness, and how to live, organized by someone else.

**The concept**
A lot landed, but three things in particular: the happiness framework — not as a feeling that arrives when things go well, but as something you build deliberately by removing wants rather than adding outcomes. The reading habit — treating books as a practice, not a task. And the idea that if what you do feels like work to others but not to you, that's the signal you're in the right place. That one I keep coming back to.

**The decision**
"Harder the workout, easier the day." I took that one literally. Physical discipline carries over — what you do to yourself in the morning sets the difficulty level of everything that follows.

**Effect**
The re-reading concept stuck. The same book hits differently at different points in life — the Almanack is one I'll come back to, the same way Meditations is. Some books are references, not one-time reads.

**Trade-off**
Naval built this over decades. Reading it in a few hours gives you the map, not the territory. The risk is feeling like you've absorbed something you've only been introduced to. Worth a re-read every year or two rather than once and done.

title: The art of war
author: Sun Tzu
lesson: If you know your enemy and yourself, you will not fear 100 battles.
notes:
**The problem**
Written thousands of years ago and still recommended today. That alone is a reason to read it. Not everything old survives — this one did for a reason.

**The concept**
The core is deceptively simple: know yourself and you have 50% chance of winning. Know yourself and the enemy, and the battle is already decided before it starts. What I took from it wasn't the military framing — it was applying that to reading myself. Self-control isn't about winning; it's about not losing by default. Most defeats are self-inflicted.

**The decision**
The distinction between a good general and a bad one stayed with me. A good general wins before the fight through preparation, intelligence, and discipline in ranks. A bad general improvises and hopes. Intelligence and espionage aren't dramatic — they're just disciplined information gathering before action.

**Effect**
I started applying the self-knowledge side more consciously — reading my own patterns the same way Sun Tzu treats knowing the terrain. Where am I weak, where do I overestimate myself, where do I move too fast without reading the situation first.

**Trade-off**
It's a short book that reads like a list of principles, not a narrative. Some chapters land hard, others need more context to apply. Worth reading slowly and more than once — each principle is a prompt to think, not an instruction to follow literally.

title: Rich dad poor dad
author: Robert Kiyosaki
lesson: If you can’t manage yourself you can’t control money
notes:
**The problem**
It’s one of the most recommended books on mindset for a reason. The reputation brought me to it — and the reputation is earned.

**The concept**
The two dads aren’t just a storytelling device. They’re two complete frameworks for how to think about money placed side by side. The protagonist’s biological father — educated, salaried, secure — represents one face of the coin. The rich dad represents the other: system thinking, cash flow, tax awareness, and the difference between working for money and having money work. What I found most useful wasn’t the asset/liability split specifically, but the reasoning behind it — how the rich dad thinks about a financial decision versus how the poor dad does.

**The decision**
The tax system section in particular changed how I look at financial structure. Not as something that happens to you, but as something you can understand and plan around. That’s the kind of thinking the book is really teaching.

**Effect**
The mental model of cash flow stuck. Every financial decision now has a side — does it produce or does it cost. That filter is simple and it works.

**Trade-off**
Kiyosaki is controversial for good reason — some of the execution advice is thin and the success stories are hard to verify. Read it for the framework, not as a playbook. The mindset shift is real. The specific steps are not always as clean as he makes them sound.

title: Purpose & Profit
author: Dan Koe
lesson:
You don’t discover purpose: you create it through action.
Clarity comes after doing, not before.
notes:
**The problem**
I followed Dan Koe on YouTube before reading this — his reasoning on AI and building pulled me in. The book is an extension of that thinking, more structured.

**The concept**
The core is three steps in sequence: make a change in yourself, craft something useful from it, sell it. That sequence is where purpose comes from — not from searching for it, but from building something real and putting it out. Koe’s argument is that meaning isn’t found, it’s constructed through the act of creating something that matters to you and is useful to someone else.

**The decision**
The honest simplicity of that formula stayed with me. Change → craft → sell. Most people skip the first step and wonder why the work feels hollow.

**Effect**
It reframed how I think about what I’m building. The goal isn’t just to ship something functional — it’s to make something that carries a point of view, something that came from a real change I went through. That’s what gives it purpose beyond the output.

**Trade-off**
Koe is his own example, which makes him convincing and also hard to benchmark against. The model works when you have something genuine to say and the patience to build an audience for it. Without either, it stays theoretical.

title: 4 Hours work week
author: Tim Ferris
lesson: Optimize what you give your attention to, not just how fast you move through it.
notes:
**The problem**
Found it online and was curious about the DEAL framework — Define, Eliminate, Automate, Liberate. I wasn't trying to escape a job. I wanted to understand how to think about where my time actually goes.

**The concept**
DEAL is the structure, but the real insight is about attention direction. Ferriss isn't saying do less — he himself grinds hard, and the book makes that obvious once you read closely. The point is to be deliberate about what gets your focus. Meaning matters more than output metrics, and most people never question whether what they're optimizing for is actually worth optimizing.

**The decision**
I shifted what I point my attention at. Less urgency-driven, more direction-driven. The VA section specifically planted a seed: any repeatable task that doesn't require judgment should be delegated or automated. That framing stuck.

**Implementation**
I started thinking about AI as a virtual assistant — not chatting with it, but actually routing low-judgment work through it the way Ferriss describes offloading to a human VA. The principle transfers cleanly.

**Effect**
Discovered meaning over pure business logic as a real signal to optimize for. That was more valuable than any productivity trick in the book.

**Trade-off**
Ferriss is a counterexample to his own thesis — he works constantly and admits it. The 4-hour week is a thought experiment, not a target. The book is most useful if you take the framework and ignore the lifestyle fantasy around it.

title: The Richest Man in Babylon
author: George S. Clason
lesson: Wealth grows wherever men exert energy.
notes:
**The problem**
You work, you earn, you spend. Month ends the same as it started. Not because you earn too little — because nothing is protected before the rest touches it.

**The concept**
Pay yourself first. Ten percent of what you earn belongs to you before it belongs to anyone else. Before rent. Before subscriptions. Before anything. Arkad — the richest man in Babylon — built everything on this single rule, and he started with nothing.

**The decision**
Set an automatic transfer the day income arrives. Not after expenses. First.

**Implementation**
One fixed amount, same day, every cycle. The size doesn't matter at the start. The habit does. You're training the behavior, not optimizing the number.

**Effect**
After a few weeks the account just exists. You stop noticing the transfer leaving. You start noticing it building. Small, but real.

**Trade-off**
It won't protect you from bad decisions with the remaining 90%. The discipline of this first step doesn't automatically teach you what to do once you have something saved. The book answers "how to start." It doesn't go much further than that.

title: The Psychology of Money
author: Morgan Housel
lesson: Knowing when you have enough is the key to lasting happiness and avoiding unnecessary risk.
notes:
**The problem**
I came to it as a finance book from someone who had actually worked in the industry. Not a theorist — someone with real exposure to how money behaves and how people behave around it.

**The concept**
The rule of enough is the core. But what made it land wasn't the abstract principle — it was a specific story in the book. A man who worked in the stock market, surrounded by people optimising for net worth, made buying a home his first financial priority. Not for investment returns. Because a safe and stable place for him and his wife was what he actually valued. That shocked me. Someone inside the machine who chose security over growth, and was at peace with that.

**The decision**
Enough isn't a number you hit. It's a state you define. That man knew what enough looked like before he had it. Most people don't define it at all and keep moving the target without realising it.

**Effect**
The contrast between what finance people are supposed to want and what that person actually chose stuck with me more than any framework in the book. It made "enough" feel like a real thing rather than a soft concept.

**Trade-off**
Housel writes in short essays and the book covers a lot of ground quickly. Some chapters land harder than others. It's not as system-focused as Rich Dad Poor Dad — it's more about behaviour than structure. Both are worth reading, for different reasons.

title: The Alchemist
author: Paulo Coelho
notes:
**The problem**
I don't usually read novels. I prefer structured books — frameworks, data, something I can extract and apply. Fiction isn't where I naturally go. I read this one anyway.

**The concept**
Maktub — "it is written." The idea that the path exists before you walk it, and the only real question is whether you follow it or stay comfortable. What stayed with me is the specific moment when Santiago had everything to stay for: a place, a life, someone he loved. He kept going anyway, knowing he might lose her. That choice — risking something real for something uncertain — is what the whole book is actually about.

**The decision**
No tactical change came from this. It's not that kind of book. What it does is make the cost of staying visible. You can see the moment Santiago could have stopped, and you feel what it would have meant if he had.

**Effect**
I came in skeptical and finished it without regret. For someone who doesn't read stories, that's worth noting.

**Trade-off**
It's a parable. It doesn't give you a plan, a framework, or a decision model. If that's what you're looking for, look elsewhere. What it gives you is a story that stays in your head longer than most structured books do.
